nativeRange.habitat: A reef-associated deepwater species, typically found over sloping rubble bottoms on outer reef slopes.

decorPreferences: live rock with caves and overhangs

diet.notes: Wild diet consists primarily of zooplankton and small crustaceans picked from the water column above reef rubble. In aquariums, generally accepts vitamin-enriched frozen mysis and brine shrimp, finely chopped seafood, and high-quality marine pellet or flake foods. Best fed multiple small feedings per day to mimic the planktivorous wild feeding pattern.

avoidWith: other Cirrhilabrus and Paracheilinus species in modest tanks

summary: Pyle's Fairy Wrasse is a vividly colored deepwater species from the Western Pacific, found across Indonesia, Papua New Guinea, the Solomon Islands, and Vanuatu. It is generally considered reef-safe and well suited to intermediate-level reef aquariums fitted with a tight lid.
careNotes: Pyle's Fairy Wrasse was named in honor of ichthyologist Richard Pyle of the Bishop Museum, who collected the specimens from which the species was first described. It is a mesophotic species — one that lives in the dimly lit 'twilight zone' between roughly 55 and 110 m depth — found over sloping rubble bottoms on outer reefs across Indonesia, Papua New Guinea, the Solomon Islands, and Vanuatu. Despite its deep-water origin, aquarium-kept specimens tend to tolerate normal reef conditions well, though fish collected from these depths undergo significant pressure changes during collection, and freshly imported individuals should be acclimated cautiously. Like all members of the genus Cirrhilabrus, this species is a protogynous sequential hermaphrodite, meaning individuals are born female and the dominant individual in a group can transition to male. In the wild they form harems consisting of one supermale and several females; in the aquarium, best results are typically achieved by keeping a single male or by introducing a male alongside one or more females at the same time. Like all fairy wrasses, Pyle's Fairy Wrasse is widely regarded as an active jumper, and a tightly sealed cover is essential. It has been reported to be aggressive toward other Cirrhilabrus and Paracheilinus wrasses, particularly in smaller systems; in reef tanks with dissimilar tankmates it tends to be considerably more peaceful. Buyers should be aware that 'Pyle's Fairy Wrasse' in the trade is occasionally confused with C. briangreenei (the Latigo Wrasse), which can be told apart by the black spot on its dorsal fin and darkened tail tip — features absent in C. pylei.
breedingNotes: Captive spawning of Cirrhilabrus pylei is not commonly reported.

primary.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
primary.notes: Primary LiveAquaria product page (https://www.liveaquaria.com/products/pyles-fairy-wrasse) returned HTTP 404 at access time; BlueZoo Aquatics (next listed saltwater fish secondary in source-urls.json) was promoted to primary. Product page used for adult size (3.5 in), minimum tank (50 gal, rounded up), care level (intermediate), temperament (peaceful with non-conspecifics), diet (carnivore), reef-safe status (yes), native range (Papua New Guinea), and jumping/escape-risk note.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-verified taxonomy (family Labridae, order Eupercaria/Labriformes), maximum size (9.0 cm TL male, 6.2 cm SL female), depth range (55–110 m, mesophotic), native range (Western Pacific: Indonesia, PNG, Solomon Islands, Vanuatu), preferred temperature 25.2–27.8°C (mean 26.5°C), reef-associated non-migratory over sloping rubble bottoms, distinct pairing during breeding, IUCN Least Concern (2009). FishBase confirms the deepwater collection origin.

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked native range (Indonesia, PNG, Solomon Islands, Vanuatu; earlier Philippine reports were misidentifications), depth range (55–82 m), maximum size (7.6–12.7 cm), peach-to-salmon overall coloration, etymology honoring Richard Pyle of the Bishop Museum, and distinguishing features versus C. briangreenei (Latigo Wrasse).

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (9 cm / 3.5 in), minimum tank (400 L / 106 gal), reef-safe status ('always reef safe'), carnivorous planktivore diet (krill, mysis, artemia, cyclops, copepods), and 'docile' temperament. ReefApp 400 L value drove the final tank-size rounding.
